title: Post-Delivery Dispute Pattern with Carrier Confirmation Mismatch
narrative: Cardholder initiates chargeback 45-75 days post-purchase claiming non-receipt despite carrier delivery confirmation to registered address. Pattern emerges with digital goods or high-value electronics ($300-$1,200 range) where merchant lacks signature requirement. Fraudster exploits maximum dispute window, filing exactly 2-3 days before 75-day threshold. TransactionAmt typically $450-$850 for apparel/electronics (ProductCD=W or C). Delivery address (addr1) matches billing in 98% of cases. Customer account age 180-365 daysâ€”old enough to appear legitimate but young enough to abandon post-dispute. P_emaildomain often generic (gmail.com, yahoo.com) with account creation within 6 months of purchase. No prior disputes on card1, establishing clean history before strike.
indicators: Chargeback filed 72-75 days post-transaction, maximizing dispute window exploitation
TransactionAmt between $450-$850 for ProductCD W (apparel) or C (electronics)
card1_txn_count_30day = 1-2, indicating infrequent usage pattern before dispute
dist1 (distance between billing/shipping) < 5km, addresses match perfectly
P_emaildomain from top-3 free providers with domain registration < 180 days prior
Delivery confirmation timestamp exists but merchant lacks adult signature requirement
Customer account age 180-365 days at transaction time with zero prior dispute history
summary: Customer files a chargeback 72-75 days after purchase claiming non-delivery, despite carrier confirmation showing delivery to their registered address. The transaction amount falls in the $450-$850 range for electronics or apparel, the account is 6-12 months old with no dispute history, and billing matches shipping address exactly.
reasoning: The fraudster deliberately waits until just before the dispute deadline to exploit the merchant's lack of signature requirement, betting they can claim non-receipt even with delivery confirmation. The clean account history and precise timing reveal calculated abuse rather than genuine customer confusion about a missing package.
indicator_explanations: Dispute filed at maximum allowable window suggesting deliberate timing strategy
Purchase amount hits sweet spot where disputes are worth effort but avoid scrutiny
Cardholder rarely uses this card making the transaction pattern look isolated
Billing and shipping addresses are virtually identical eliminating mismatch defense
Email uses throwaway provider created recently specifically for this purchase cycle
Package was delivered but no adult signature captured to prove receipt
Account matured just long enough to appear legitimate before filing first dispute
